questions.text: What does enabling 'pipelining = True' in ansible.cfg do?

options.text: Enables parallel task execution within a single host
Reduces SSH connections by sending multiple operations in one connection

questions.explanation: SSH pipelining reduces the number of SSH connections by sending multiple operations over a single connection, avoiding the overhead of establishing a new connection per task. It requires that requiretty is disabled in sudoers on managed nodes. This can give 2-5x speedup on large inventories.

questions.text: What does the 'forks' setting control in Ansible?

options.text: The number of tasks that run in parallel on a single host
The number of hosts Ansible communicates with simultaneously
The number of retries for failed tasks
The number of Ansible processes spawned on the control node

questions.explanation: The forks setting (default: 5) controls how many hosts Ansible communicates with in parallel. Increasing forks (e.g., forks = 50) dramatically speeds up large deployments. Balance against available CPU/memory on the control node and network bandwidth.

questions.text: What does 'strategy: free' do differently from the default 'linear' strategy?

options.text: Skips failed hosts without stopping the play
Each host moves to the next task as soon as it finishes the current one, without waiting for other hosts
Runs all tasks on all hosts at the same time

questions.explanation: With strategy: free, each host proceeds to its next task immediately after completing the current one, without waiting for slower hosts. This means faster hosts complete the play earlier. The linear strategy (default) synchronizes all hosts at each task boundary.

questions.text: What does 'poll: 0' mean in an async task?

options.text: The task will be checked 0 times
The task is launched and Ansible moves on immediately without waiting
The task polls for completion every 0 milliseconds (busy-wait)

questions.explanation: poll: 0 means 'fire and forget' — Ansible starts the task and immediately moves on without waiting for it to complete. Use ansible.builtin.async_status later to check on the job. This is useful for launching multiple long-running operations in parallel.

questions.text: Which gather_facts setting only collects facts if they are not already cached?
questions.explanation: gathering = smart in ansible.cfg makes Ansible only gather facts if they're not already in the fact cache. When combined with a fact_caching backend (jsonfile, redis, memcached), this avoids redundant SSH connections for fact gathering on repeat runs.

questions.text: What callback plugin shows per-task execution timing?
questions.explanation: The ansible.posix.profile_tasks callback plugin displays the time taken by each task at the end of a playbook run. Enable it in ansible.cfg: callbacks_enabled = ansible.posix.profile_tasks. The ansible.posix.timer plugin shows total playbook runtime.

questions.text: What is the purpose of 'gather_subset' in a play?

options.text: Filters which hosts facts are gathered from
Collects only specific categories of facts instead of all facts
Gathers facts from a subset of inventory groups
Enables partial fact gathering for Windows hosts only

questions.explanation: gather_subset limits which fact modules run. Use gather_subset: ['!all', 'network'] to gather only network facts. This speeds up plays that only need specific facts. The '!all' prefix disables all defaults, then you add back only what you need.

questions.text: What module checks the status of an async job launched with poll: 0?
questions.explanation: ansible.builtin.async_status checks the status of a previously launched async task. Provide the jid (job ID) from the registered variable of the async task. Use with until: result.finished and retries/delay to poll until completion.

questions.text: Which fact caching backend is recommended for distributed Ansible control nodes?
questions.explanation: Redis is recommended when running multiple Ansible control nodes or AWX/Ansible Automation Platform workers, since it provides a shared cache accessible by all nodes. jsonfile caching is local-only and doesn't work across multiple control nodes.

questions.text: What is the maximum runtime of an async task with 'async: 600'?
questions.explanation: The async value specifies the maximum runtime in seconds. async: 600 means the task can run for up to 10 minutes. If the task exceeds this time, Ansible will kill it. Set async high enough for your longest expected runtime, and use poll/async_status to check progress.
